title: Discovery of a nonsymmorphic superconductor with spontaneous rotational symmetry breaking and nontrivial zero modes
authors: Hui Guo, Zhixuan Li, Senhao Lv, Tianqi Gao, Zihao Huang, Kuanrong Hao, Lizhi Zhang, Ke Zhu, Siyu Li, Xianghe Han, Xiao Lin, Shengshan Qin, Wu Zhou, Haitao Yang, Hui Chen, Hong-Jun Gao
abstract: Topological superconductivity has attracted great interest due to its fundamental significance for realizing Majorana quasiparticles and fault-tolerant quantum computation. Nonsymmorphic superconductors, with symmetry-protected nontrivial electronic structures, offer a promising route to exotic topological superconducting states, yet experimental realizations remain scarce. Here we identify nonsymmorphic compound PtPb4 as a robust platform hosting superconductivity with spontaneous rotational symmetry breaking and nontrivial zero-energy modes. PtPb4 crystallizes in a frustrated Shastry-Sutherland lattice and exhibits nontrivial band topology. By combining in-plane and out-of-plane resistivity measurements, pronounced twofold anisotropy is observed in both the superconducting state and the upper critical field, evidencing spontaneous rotational symmetry breaking. Scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy further reveal twofold-symmetric magnetic vortices, providing direct real-space evidence for the symmetry-broken superconducting state. Notably, a robust zero-energy vortex bound state emerges and persists without spatial splitting over extended distances, consistent with the characteristics expected for Majorana bound state. These findings uncover an exotic superconducting state in PtPb4 and establish a promising platform for exploring topological superconductivity and superconducting quantum devices.
summary: This paper identifies PtPb4 as a unique nonsymmorphic superconductor that spontaneously breaks the rotational symmetry of its underlying crystal lattice. The researchers provide direct real-space evidence of elliptical magnetic vortices and robust zero-energy states, positioning this material as a promising platform for Majorana-based topological quantum computing.
main_problem: The difficulty in experimentally realizing and identifying intrinsic topological superconductivity in bulk crystals, specifically finding platforms that exhibit spontaneous symmetry breaking and robust topological zero modes.
main_result: The discovery of spontaneous rotational symmetry breaking (from C4 to C2) in the superconductor PtPb4, accompanied by the observation of robust, unsplit zero-energy vortex bound states consistent with Majorana physics.
method: The study combines single-crystal growth via the self-flux method, structural characterization (XRD, STEM, EELS), transport measurements (angle-dependent magnetoresistance), and low-temperature STM/STS spectroscopy, supported by DFT calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is PtPb4, a nonsymmorphic compound with a tetragonal space group (P4/nbm) featuring a frustrated Shastry-Sutherland lattice and nontrivial band topology.
key_observables: Twofold anisotropy in in-plane and c-axis resistivity, anisotropic magnetoresistance (AMR) ratio, elliptical vortex core morphology, and zero-energy vortex bound states in dI/dV spectra.
important_parameters: Tc approx 2.85 K, superconducting gap approx 0.42 meV, AMR ratio of ~194%, and vortex core anisotropy ratio (xi_a/xi_b) of ~1.8.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the observed symmetry breaking is intrinsic to the superconducting state rather than a structural phase transition, which is verified by STM imaging.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the tetragonal structure, XRD, and atomic-resolution STEM/EELS; Figure 2 illustrates twofold symmetric superconductivity via angle-dependent resistivity and polar plots of magnetoresistance; Figure 3 focuses on c-axis resistivity and vortex properties.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural confirmation of the PtPb4 crystal lattice, moves to transport measurements demonstrating spontaneous rotational symmetry breaking, presents STM/STS evidence of elliptical vortices and zero-energy modes, and concludes with the implications for topological superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
